I found those kids very interesting and thought it was an interesting idea for the movie. I haven’t read the book but I’m pretty sure they didn’t have animal of the corn kids in it, but I like when directors take different approaches and diff ideas to a well known property and they didn’t really take advantage of that, . text: His wife didn't want him to publish pet semetary. It was a real road they lived on and his daughters cat really got killed on it. They had small children. author: @TheAgame914

text: as someone whos read the book and loved the first movie, this thing is going to be terrible. theyve shown in their trailer that they have removed everything that made the original film truly frightening and replaced it with a bunch of cliches. Zelda, in the film, was played my a man, in order to give it a physically warped and psychologically disturbing feel. and it worked. it worked so well, they used the same tactic in the film, The Ring, with Samara, with similar results. But now, theyre acting like thats an amateur approach. So, they just cast a little girl and its not going to feel like anything else but a little girl. its going to be sad, not bone chilling... They opted for some pretty cat, made to look menacing with cgi, insted of using a real cat that looks and sounds menacing on its own. They wanted a pretty cat. this already tells me they are relying on looks and cgi to carry the film, and ignoring what makes the book and film truly scary, the realism and depth... Using Ellie as the child Louis loses, insted of Gage, well, come on, how gut wrenching was it to see a toddler die by running out into the road, chasing a kite? theres no way using Ellie is going to lend more to the emotional power of the story. That moment in the original film haunted people for a lifetime. They wanted to use Ellie, so they could give the dead child more depth and more to do, well, thats just lazy filmaking. because you dont need to add more PLOT to a BOOK with plot to spare. And it was the subtlety that made Gage so scary. He was not that mobile, but his parents grief caused them to welcome the little zombie into their arms without fear of consequences. thats the point of the book. thats the point of the whole story. how disarmed a grieving parent becomes to logic and danger, in the midst of their bereavement. i cringe at the thought of what they want to make of Ellies character. i just know its going to be garbage... People keep mentioning "the wendigo", like we are going to get to see the wendigo, well, the wendigo is not a central theme of the book. the book does not make the wendigo a character, it is a passing reference to mythology. and barely focused on to any substantial degree with concern for the plot. im like, this story does not NEED a MONSTER to make it scary or carry out its design. it is scary enough as it is. and how could they even have enough film time to add more story or plot elements? the problem with adapting books to film is you have too much story and not enough screen time. so what are they thinking? seriously?.... and the fact that they are just opting not to let Judd have a new england accent, that is what painted the eerie tone of Judds character in the book and movie. It was creepy. the accent gets into your psyche and lends to the ambiance of the story. paints a mood. theres no reason not to have it, unless John Lithgow just cant do a new england accent for some reason. but its an easy accent, and whats the problem...... also, what is the deal with the kids wearing animal masks? thats nowhere in the book. are they trying to combine children of the corn for flair, or tongue in cheek, since its stephen king? UNNECESSARY. again, this story is scary enough without all this garbage. and i doubt theyll have time to flesh out relationships between main characters with all this "reboot" filler garbage. theres no cult of children in the book, or first film. and it wouldnt make sense if there was. its not part of the story. so how are they going to add that without it being pointless?... and pascow and timmy were two very crucial elements in the story and first film, that were already done to perfection. changing pascows character(im not talking about his race, but his origin. making him something more powerful and wise.) and omitting the story of timmy is totally needless. pascow is already everything the story needs him to be. why would you add extra layers when the story doesnt require it?...... Also, why show the scene where Judd gets his achilles tendon cut IN THE TRAILER??? are you trying to give the entire movie away? That scene in the first film was a huge punch to the psyche, and youre just going to waste that moment for a gag in the preview or some kind of throwback nod? break the fourth wall some more why dont you. do you even care about making a film thats going to be scary, or is this a joke? because it looks like a joke. it looks like that is the whole point of this effort, to make fun of the original film and book asif they are basic. man, you cant hold a candle to the original. you dont understand HALF of what is necessary to do what the originals did and  i feel embarrassed for you.

text: Much of Stephen King's book was based upon personal experience. There was a trucking facility that drove their large trucks up and down the highway where he was living in Maine and many animals were in fact killed. Kings's daughter had a cat named Smuckey who died and was buried in the pet cemetery. The children started the cemetery, but misspelled it Sematary. King himself had a near tragedy when his son ran towards the road and he was able to grab him at the last second. King began to ask the questions what if my daughter's cat could come back from the dead and what if I hadn't grabbed my son from the road in time?

text:
I've always liked the novel and i thought the 1989 adaptation was more than decent.
In fact, along with Cujo and Christine, one of the most faithful as well as enjoyable King adaptation there is of his purely horror novels (Misery is more of a thriller and an excellent one at that, and shining, while unsurpassed, is too different from the book).

So of course, i'm eagerly waiting for this new take.
On the original adaptation, Fred Gwynne was fantastic as Jud Crandall, he nailed the Maine accent and was all around likable.
The scenes with Zelda (played by a male actor) were truly unnerving.
The only thing missing was the story of the Wendigo which is the scariest part in an already scary book.

The trailer looks good, storywise, and John Litgow is always a welcomed addition, he's so talented.

I hope they won't back down from the gore or scares as Stephen King himself admitted Pet Sematary was his scariest novel, though i am worried because it was said there would be some story changes and that is not always a good thing, though, it is reported that King approved those changes.

So all in all, Pet Sematary promises to be one of the most anticipated horror films of 2019, let's hope it lives up to the hype.

author: @abearah9255

text:
People here are so busy saying so much from the book was left out of the original movie. What it seems, most of you are completely unaware of, is that Stephen King himself wrote the screenplay for the original movie. Also you cant take 600 pages of a novel and put all of that in a movie unless you want the movie to be 3 hours long. And Stephen King even had a cameo as a priest. It had that King feeling about it, because King was so involved in it. They litterally shot the movie on loacation in Maine, 5 minutes from where King lives. You cant beat that. This movie was shot in Canada. 

And from what I understand they changed everything this time around. Gage doesnt die, his sister does. Not to mention the kids with the animal masks that was not in the book, nor in the original movie. And probably many more things.

And yes this is a remake of the original movie. It's not a re-adaptation of the book. Only the original movie is and can be an adaptation of the book. That goes for all movies based on books. Only the first movies can be called adaptations. You cant just change a fact like that, because it suits you, to make this probably shitty remake seem like a new fresh movie.

text:
As much as I love the original, this seems like it will be more accurate to the book. Church is a tabby this time around, as he is in the book. Also, from the looks of things, they aren't going with the "evil zombie cat", like they did in the OG movie, and are instead choosing to make Church just seem like a menacing force, looking on with a twisted sense of hate. They include the symbol of the wendigo, it looks like Norma Crandall will be included, and hell, they even have the loon cry from the book!

 That isn't to say the original movie isn't any good. The location and setting of the original movie looks closer to what I would have imagined in the book (this new setting seems like your average haunted forest type). Plus, Fred Gwynne as Jud is perfect casting. Don't get me wrong, if anyone could do a good job with it, it's John Lithgow, but I seriously doubt he will ever be able to match Gwynnes performance. 

All in all, I'm very excited for this.

text:
Man, imagine being someone who:
Judges a movie and says it’s sucks before it had even come out.
Calls this a remake when it’s a new adaptation of the book.
Complains about certain actors being in a movie after they have only heard a small bit of dialogue from them.
Claiming that “their going to ruin the original for me with this one!” when it’s a new adaptation, that you don’t have to watch, and is purposely meant to be different from the 1989 adaptation.

“It” was a new adaptation that took plenty of liberties and it was pretty good and most would agree that it was better than the miniseries.  This, like “It” will take some liberties and I’m all for it.  It isn’t going to be the 1989 movie beat for beat because it’s based off the same source but is a new vision of it.   How about people stop being so quick to judge this based off one trailer and little other information while acting like they’ve see the movie already.

Note: I have seen the ‘89 version and in my opinion it didn’t live up to the hype people surrounded it with and I didn’t think it lived up to its full potential.  I’d love to see what a new adaptation could bring.  I doubt it could live up to the book, but movies rarely ever.  It’s hard to characterize characters like Louis as good as in a book because you are in his head the entire way, but I think Jason Clarke will do fine.

text:
Fun fact: I have the script to the new movie, I work in the business, and if you want to know the changes, SPOILER below:



Gage goes into the road during the house party, and before the truck can hit him just in time, Rachel saves him, leaving Ellie all alone. The cat Church then goes into the road to lure Ellie, and Ellie is hit by the truck! This is real because in the script it includes the children with drums, and that shot of the cat in the road is where Ellie dies. Ellie is then resurrected, and rather than Louis die at the end, it's Rachel. The spinning girl we see in the trailer is the undead Ellie, who twirls around in the script.

Proof is how in the script, there are a lot of scenes in here that are in it, like the kids with drums, and the elevator shaft. Those hands in the trailer are to big to belong to a toddler, and that coffin in the trailer is WAY too big for a toddler, and would belong to someone the size of Ellie.

There's this whole thing in the script with black pupils and irises, and if you look closely in the trailer, Church in Ellie's room has a black pupil and a black iris.

text:
It's a bit funny how some people are so upset and almost take it personally that they are doing a re-adaption of the book, just because "the original movie was great". Like, no one is forcing you to watch it? And if you do watch and don't like it, just rewatch the original then? lol
I agree, the original was awesome and gave me nightmares all through my childhood and even still today (i'm 27)! Still, i can't wait to watch this movie, i doubt that it will be as scary as the first one (especially Zelda) but i'm still really excited about it. I read the book last year and realized there was so much more to the story than the movie let us see. I hope they've managed to capture more of the psychological horror from the book in this readaption!

text:
I always thought the "scariest" scenes in the 1989 version were everywhere and constant. 

The beginning with children's voices grieving over dead pets, the amazing detail and look of the pet sematary in a spiral, a dead and blood clotted apparition named Pascow appearing in the night to warn about the "sour" ground and to not cross the boundary. ... And that's in the first ten minutes. 
The amazing lonely and desolate but very important look and feel of the Micmac burial ground was perfection and Timmy Baterman's story scared me silly.
 Sure my childish youth made it that much scarier, but it left a powerful impact. 
Have read the book countless times and can only hope a remake is worthy.

text: WTF???? Here was a NEW chance to improve upon the original '89 film adaptation of S. King's greatest straight up horror novel, and it's obvious they're already tossing in garbage that has NOTHING to do with the book. What's with the kids in the masks??? The girl with the drum??? And you can tell that Jud is very poorly cast. He doesn't seem anything like the tough, old, wise geezer from the novel. And just that cheap "jump-scare" of the truck blasting by on the highway...gimme a freakin' break. WHY? Why for once can't someone (other than Kubrick, who I doubt is available-RIP) do an intelligent adaptation of King's novels? King should have almost NOTHING to do with anything other than maybe the screenplay adaptation. I'm not sure how much (if any) King had to do with this film, but his strength lay in writing, NOT movie making. Oh man, if they just could stop making these IQ32 level films and make an intelligent film. 120 minutes with no gimmicks, just the cold harsh realities of the novel and its message that death is certain and life is not. ARGHHHH, this just pisses me off. Hollywood has reached rock bottom and it's so sad. To think of the masterpieces of the late-60's through even the 80's (but especially the 70's, of course). Nearly EVERYTHING on a screen today is f'ing rehashed garbage made for people too dull to read a book and far too willing to eat and watch pure shite.

text:
*Spoilers “I read the book and watched the original movie for new comers.” *







So basically, the main character moves into his new home with his family. His cat churchhill gets run over by a car so his neighbor who’s been living in that area for a long time takes him into the cemetery. Their is a separate cursed cemetery that broken down logs lead to which is an ancient Indian burial ground. I have no idea what those masked kids are there’s for since it’s not in the book. He then buries his cat with his neighbor Judd. The next day his cat comes back to life, but different. It has been possessed by an evil spirit. After, the protagonist job is a doctor so then he has to treat a case on one of his patients who got hit by a car. A spirit now in contact with him after he visited the cemetery now possessed his dead patient calling to him, and wanting him to go back to the cemetery for some reason. It then stops possession. His son, then gets hit by the truck in the beginning, foreshadowing what will eventually happen while he was at a picnic with his neighbor Judd. The protagonist then gets the stupid reason to bury his son in the ancient burial grounds, which he does. His son then comes back, but evil and now possessed. His son goes to Judd’s house and then proceeds to kill him. The mother wanders in seeing her son, and that he’s alive. In a trance thinking he’s alright, her son proceeds to kill her. The protagonist then kills his son and burns down the house, and takes his wife to the cemetery to bring her back. He does and then she kills her husband. Now leaving the daughter Eileen an orphan. The end, hope you enjoy the movie since I sure will.

author: @Gunlord

text: I have read all of Stephen Kings books, some of them several times, since the 1970's, up until the mid 2000's or so. I have probably read The Stand, even the unabridged version once, at least 10 times. Even the magazine stories, comic book types, his magazine from the 80's that I can't remember the name of,  Danse Macbre and even his wife Tabby's  book. I loved his introductions welcoming you to his stories. Dear Constant Reader, is how it started. His epilogues were just as great. But in his later book's, it didn't feel like his style or writing. I haven't read any of his book's since The Cell. It didn't feel like his. At first glance it does, but then you lose the King bond somewhere in it. Some of the newer ones do not have a personal invitation to join him in the books horror filled pages in his 'Dear Constant Reader' prologue. He was my favorite writer for 30 some odd years and something happened to his writing style. It's like it's not him.  I can't even read them now. I miss the excitement of waiting for his newest book. Heck, I could even afford the hardback ones now and wouldn't have to wait for the paperback. I  even refused to watch any of his movies before I had read the book. And the books were always 100 times better.

text:
WHY is everyone below going on and on about " the original"? The first movie was AWFUL. PERIOD. The bokk was absolutely terrifying: the imagery, the sleep walk "nightmare" thart wasn't a dream at all, and the ENDING...OMG! The little boy in thr mivie was, ummm, intellectually challenged, whiny voiced, and basically a cartoon that was dropped in from anothrr picture. THIS trailer, at lest, captures  the ominous, forboding doom of thr book thst the movie never even caught a glimpse of, let alone the creepy clue-by-clue becoming reality. If you want to be scared, read the book.
PS: ditto on "IT"...SUCH a stupid adaptation of the book. The clown was a bad joke, not a terrifying demonic entity. That stupid Five- Head! And those TEETH. Nowhere in the book.

text:
I never saw the original, though I read the book. I'm excited to see whether or not they do a good job with this one. On the one hand, remakes can (and often do) fall flat. On the other hand, look at what they did with IT! Personally, I considered that a great remake, partly because of all the callbacks to the book.

If there's one Stephen King story that I would like to see remade, it's The Shining. Yes, yes, I know. The original was an AMAZING MOVIE. It's a classic. But it's also not a great representation of the book. I would love to see a remake that doesn't try to one-up the original movie, but instead tries something totally different, and really stays true to the novel.

text: If there was ever a Stephen King film that needs a reboot it's Thinner in my opinion. They could've done so much with the story by making it into a deep, sorrowful drama about an ordinary man afflicted with a bizarre medical condition that makes him rapidly lose weight. It could've been a film that made you cry and feel sorry for the main character, focusing on his deterioration and the profound effect of the Gypsy curse. If you've ever seen Into The Wild, there's a scene at the end that shows the character completely emaciated and barely able to stand. It was a very sad scene. Thinner could've been done in the same way with more focus on the plight of the character instead of cheap over the top schlock accompanied by terrible acting.

text: It's not like S. King needs the money.  For that matter, usually when an author sells the rights to their books to movie studios, they get a lump sum and are done, regardless of how much the movie makes.  Now, I see King as "executive producer" on a lot of things these days, which means he's helping to fund the project and hoping for a good profit at the other end.  But, you know, I've been a huge Stephen King fan (novels) since the beginning (mid-70s) and have read all of them to date.  Some of the movies and TV shows have been good, others dreadful.  I'm waiting for that really great 'Salem's Lot remake.

text:
I grew up in Ludlow.
This story always freakin me out as a kid.
We had an old pet cemetery about 2 miles in the woods behind the farm house too.  That I built up after finding and put all our Huskies in the ground myself. 
We also had moved to Ludlow in the first place because my Grandfather opened up his own pharmaceutical business in town.
I knew this was just a book but the possibility that these stories were from real life and the fact I was actually living it made it hard to separate.
I was terrified and thrilled to spend time in the cemetery as a child because of this book.  
Such a weird experience and hard to explain.  
It was like Stephen King wrote about my family but just added zombies.

text:
Dear precious one, it is so important for you to know that Jesus Christ is your refuge, strength and an ever-present Help in times of trouble. Therefore, you don't need to be afraid of anything -- not even cataclysmic circumstances. The media are increasingly devoted to fear-inducing subject matter: terrorism, serial killers, environmental catastrophes. 

If you focus on such dangers and forget that Jesus Christ is your Refuge in all circumstances, you will become increasingly fearful. Every day He manifests His grace in countless places and situations, but the media takes no notice. Jesus showers not only blessings but also outright miracles on our planet. As you grow closer to Jesus Christ, He will open your eyes to see more and more of His presence all around you. Things that most people hardly notice, like shifting shades of sunlight, fill you with heart-bursting  joy. 

You have eyes to see and ears that hear, so proclaim Jesus Christ's abiding presence in the world this very moment by first acknowledging you are a sinner in need of our Savior. Welcome the righteousness of Jesus Christ into your life. Receive grace through faith by believing in Jesus Christ and what He did on the cross, His death, burial and resurrection. You will then be sealed and justified in salvation.  You will be rewarded greatly and your life will be forever changed! God bless you in the Glorious name of Jesus Christ our Lord and our Savior.❤️❤️❤️      (Psalm 46 : 1-3;  Psalm 89 : 15).

text:
I'm cautiously optimistic. Though, nostalgia or not, I have to be honest in that I prefer Fred Gwynne as Jud. John Lithgow doesn't have the same kind of command or quirkiness to his voice and comes across too "matter of fact" in how he speaks. It looks & sounds like Lithgow playing a character.

Gwynne carried more of a country folk eeriness to him as someone who'd lived in the area for so long. He felt like a natural part of the setting, not an actor playing an exposition giver. I'll try to reserve judgment but first impressions are important and Lithgow's version isn't off to the best start for me.

text: This looks like it could be good but, certain parts stick out to me that it's going to go for a "modern" sense of horror. I hate the kids in the animal masks because "creepy kids" have become such a mundane staple in today's horror films. They just aren't scary and I love the book and the first two movies. In fact the first movie seems more closer to the novel than this one does. I loved the original movie and I was hoping this adaptation would be closer to King's novel. It looks like it'll be more like the first movie but with a few differences. The original movie also felt more close to how I envisioned the characters when reading the novel. This movie looks like it'll be okay, but I'm worried that's what it's going to be. Just okay. When I want it to be as good as the first movie or the book. I also really liked Pet Sematary 2, for what it was. So, hopefully this turns out just as good as them, but I have my doubts. The casting for Louis seems way off to me though. Lithgow should do fine as Jud, but I don't know. Church looks just like the cat on the cover of the novel, so that's kind of cool.

text: There is absolutely no justifiable reason to remake the original when the original easily stands on it's own.  If you want to remake old Stephen King stuff, focus on the made for TV movies/miniseries that had major limitations forced upon them, limiting the material from the books that they could use. text:
What I liked: Production values. What I didn't like: It seems like they are trying too hard to make it creepy whhen the genius in SK is the simplicity of the storytelling. All those Stephen King B Movies are missing good writing and some money. But they do have the simplicity and they are creepy because of that. The original Pet Sematary is a good movie and faithful to the novel but Kings screenplay needed more detail and the film a longer running time.

And a little bit of money. Other than that it is faithful and a good adaptation.

Now they have the money and they exagerate. They try to make it overly creepy and overly atmospheric.


What to make a good SK film? Write with the spirit of SK, keep it simple and hire good actors and invest in effects when those are needed.

text:
Paramount & filmmakers - I hope some of you read this:
I'm a gigantic fan of Pet Sematary and just got finished hearing the newly released audiobook, read by Michael C. Hall, which was absolutely intense, hellish and mercilessly tragic! I've been anticipating the new trailer like crazy, to see what kind of "feel" it would throw off. The movie was (and I still want it to be) one of my most anticipated of 2019! But I just saw the trailer and I have to ask something: WHY put so much effort in to showing off kids in animal-like masks? What's up with those masks coming in to people's homes? Why is that the focus here? Where's the book in all of this? In this trailer, it's as if the kids are drawn to the Pet Sematary for some evil hellish reason, which in the book, not to neglect the old movie either, is NOT the case. It's a place of rest, "build by brokenhearted children". Never though, did it draw in kids for any evil or manipulating reasons - It's a good place, it's a safe place. The Micmac-burial ground BEHIND the pet sematary is the evil place. That's the irony, hidden within Kings pages. The Pet Sematary seems like a scary place but really, it is not. It's what lies further behind that's evil. It honestly seems like they're changing up that angle, and if so, it's quite simply unacceptable, no matter what new take they're trying to spin on it. "The barrier was not meant to be crossed", in the words of a certain Victor Pascow.. This trailer here, I'm sorry to say, makes it seem as if the filmmakers, whom said this new adaptation would be much truer towards King's book, don't really know what they're dealing with? I'm seriously not meaning to bash any one or to be a disrespectful dick about it :-( It's just the first impression I'm getting (and yes, I know it's just a trailer - but still) There's so much focus on kids in damn animal-masks, why? It felt like a movie from 2008, starring Liv Tyler, called "The Strangers". This trailer here, sadly, also seems to rely HEAVY on those typical and way played out "jerk-motion-effects"; something "The Ring" & "Grudge" made famous 15 some years ago :-( I pray this was only something created for the trailer... Where's the DEAD people in this thing? Where's the atmosphere? Where's the main-theme? The main theme here (again) seems to be kids in animal-masks? I don't mean to be harsh (in fact, I HATE to come off that way) but damn. It's like it's not even a trailer for Pet Sematary and just some other random "Sinister 3" movie, or something :-( That being said, it's clear that they're holding back, I can detect that much and I'm perfectly fine with such things - in fact, I admire it. I was just praying for a more Pet Sematary-like trailer for the movie, based upon the book :-( I'm not saying the filmmakers don't necessarily know what they're doing, cause the movie might still turn out great when seen in its entirety. I'm simply saying, based upon the trailer here, it seems to me that they're missing the mark by a landslide, focusing on kids in animal-masks, filling the whole trailer - as if they're the main villains? :-( Honestly, I hope to God that I'll end up eating back my words, cause I'm rooting for this film to succeed like a mad-man!! But for this first peak at things to come, I'm sorely disappointed (The actors all seem very solid, but that's beside the point)

text:
I like what they've done with Church here.  I felt, reading the book, that how he's depicted in this trailer, scruffy, something seems off is how he was meant to appear.  In the original movie, it was clear from the moment he came back that he was evil.  It's much more subtle in the book.  All the characters feel something is off about the cat, but he really doesn't show his true colors until the climax.

The kids in masks are replacements for the Native Americans.  It's a PC move, through and through.

Diesel truck jump scare not only works, but is canon to the book!  The area is literally described as shown in the trailer.  The trucking road is a major discussion point for all the adult characters, and a major plot point as well.

I get the feeling this is going to be nicely terrifying as a movie, and while the original is campy fun, I'd love to see an adaption as incredibly dark as the book, which is what this appears to be going for.
